questions.specification: Yejin plans to retire at age 60. She wants to create an annuity fund, which will pay her a monthly allowance of \$4000 at the end of each month during her retirement. She wants to save enough money so that the payments last for 30 years. A financial advisor has told her that she can expect to earn 5% interest on her funds, compounded annually.

parts.content: Calculate the amount Yejin needs to have saved into her annuity fund, in order to meet her retirement goal. Give your answer to the nearest dollar.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to use financial solver or analytic method <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify correct parameters: $N=360$, $I\%=5$, $PMT=4000$, $FV=0$, $P/Y=12$, $C/Y=1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the present value: $PV = 754\,638$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
Yejin wants to find out how much money she needs at the beginning of her retirement to receive a monthly allowance of $4000$ for $30$ years. Since she is withdrawing money from a fund that earns interest, this is an **annuity** problem. 

We will use the **GDC Financial Solver** (often called the TVM Solver) to find the **Present Value (PV)**, which represents the lump sum she needs to have saved by age 60.

steps.title: Define solver parameters
steps.content:
To use the financial solver, we need to identify the following variables based on the question:

*   $N$: The total number of payment periods. Since there are 12 months in a year and the payments last for 30 years:
    $$N = 30 \times 12 = 360$$
*   $I\%$: The annual interest rate.
    $$I\% = 5$$
*   $PV$: The amount we are solving for (the initial savings).
*   $PMT$: The amount paid out each month. We enter this as a positive value if we consider it money Yejin receives.
    $$PMT = 4000$$
*   $FV$: The future value. She wants the money to last exactly 30 years, so the balance will be zero at the end.
    $$FV = 0$$
*   $P/Y$: Number of payments per year.
    $$P/Y = 12$$
*   $C/Y$: Number of compounding periods per year. The interest is compounded **annually**.
    $$C/Y = 1$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 30 * 12.

ti84.instructions: Enter 30 * 12 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Calculate 30 * 12.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of periods N.

steps.title: Solve for Present Value
steps.content:
Now, enter these values into your calculator's financial solver and solve for $PV$. 

Note: In most calculators, $PV$ will appear as a negative number (e.g., $-754\,638.16...$) because it represents the "outflow" of money into the fund at the start to allow for the future "inflows" of monthly payments.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Finance or TVM menu. Enter the parameters and solve for PV.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS], select 'Finance', then 'TVM Solver'. Enter the values and press [ALPHA][SOLVE] on the PV line.

desmos.instructions:
Financial solvers are not native to Desmos, but the formula for the present value of an ordinary annuity with different compounding is:
PV = PMT * [(1 - (1 + r/k)^(-kn)) / ((1 + r/k)^(1/m) - 1)] where m is payments per year and k is compounding per year. Here, it's easier to use the GDC approach as requested.

calculator.description: Use the TVM solver to find PV with N=360, I=5, PMT=4000, FV=0, P/Y=12, C/Y=1.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a value of approximately $754\,638.16...$. 

The question asks for the answer to the **nearest dollar**.

$$PV \approx 754\,638$$

steps.title: Identify payment details
steps.content:
First, we determine the total number of payments Yejin will receive. She wants to receive a monthly allowance for 30 years.

Since there are 12 months in a year, the total number of payments ($n$) is:
$$n = 30 \times 12 = 360 \text{ payments}$$

steps.title: Find monthly interest rate
steps.content:
The interest rate is given as 5% compounded annually. To find the present value of monthly payments, we need to calculate the equivalent effective monthly interest rate ($i$).

The annual growth factor is $1 + 0.05 = 1.05$. We need to find $i$ such that applying it 12 times results in this annual growth:
$$(1 + i)^{12} = 1.05$$

Solving for $i$:
$$i = (1.05)^{\frac{1}{12}} - 1 \approx 0.004074...$$

steps.title: Set up geometric series
steps.content:
The amount Yejin needs today is the sum of the **present values** of all 360 future payments. A payment of $4000$ received at the end of month $k$ has a present value of $4000(1+i)^{-k}$.

The total present value ($PV$) is the sum of this geometric series:
$$PV = 4000(1+i)^{-1} + 4000(1+i)^{-2} + \dots + 4000(1+i)^{-360}$$

From the series, we identify:
- First term ($u_1$): $$4000(1+i)^{-1}$$
- Common ratio ($r$): $$(1+i)^{-1}$$
- Number of terms ($n$): $$360$$

highlights.text: at the end of each month

steps.title: Sum the geometric series
steps.content:
We use the sum of a geometric series formula from the data booklet:

$$\text{Geometric: } S_{n}=\frac{u_{1}(r^{n}-1)}{r-1}$$

Substituting our values:
$$PV = \frac{4000(1+i)^{-1} \left( ((1+i)^{-1})^{360} - 1 \right)}{(1+i)^{-1} - 1}$$

This can be simplified for easier calculation:
$$PV = \frac{4000(1 - (1+i)^{-360})}{i}$$

steps.title: Calculate and round
steps.content:
Now we substitute $i = (1.05)^{\frac{1}{12}} - 1$ and calculate the total amount:

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= \frac{4000(1 - (1.05^{\frac{1}{12}})^{-360})}{(1.05^{\frac{1}{12}} - 1)} \\ PV &= \frac{4000(1 - 1.05^{-30})}{1.05^{\frac{1}{12}} - 1} \\ PV &\approx 754\,638.388... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to the nearest dollar as requested, we get $754\,638$.

ti84.instructions: Enter the formula directly on the home screen. Use the fractional template (ALPHA -> Y= -> ENTER) for clarity.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula into the expression bar. Desmos will provide the decimal result immediately.

parts.content:
Yejin has just turned 28 years old. She currently has no retirement savings. She wants to save part of her salary at the end of each month into her annuity fund.

Calculate the amount Yejin needs to save each month, to meet her retirement goal given the same annual interest and annual compounding. Give your answer to the nearest dollar.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to use financial solver or analytic method <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify correct parameters: $N=384$, $I\%=5$, $PV=0$, $FV=754\,638$, $P/Y=12$, $C/Y=1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the monthly payment: $PMT = 817$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept $N=384$ or equivalent calculation $(60 - 28) \times 12$. Allow use of unrounded answer from Part 1.</note>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we need to find the monthly savings amount (**PMT**) required to grow a fund from \$0 to the target amount calculated in Part 1 (\$754,638) over the years remaining until Yejin's retirement. 

This is a classic application of the **GDC TVM Solver**, which is covered in **Topic 1.7: Loan repayments and amortization** (and general financial applications).

highlights.text: Calculate the amount Yejin needs to save each month

steps.title: Calculate the total periods
steps.content:
First, we determine how many months Yejin has until she retires at age 60. Since she is currently 28, the number of years is:

$$60 - 28 = 32 \text{ years}$$

Since she makes monthly payments, the total number of periods ($N$) is:

$$N = 32 \times 12 = 384$$

steps.title: Set TVM Solver parameters
steps.content:
Now we enter the known values into the TVM Solver on the graphing calculator. 

*   $N = 384$ (total monthly payments)
*   $I\% = 5$ (annual interest rate)
*   $PV = 0$ (she starts with no savings)
*   $FV = 754,638$ (the target amount needed at retirement)
*   $P/Y = 12$ (payments per year are monthly)
*   $C/Y = 1$ (interest is compounded annually)

Note that $FV$ is positive because it is the money she will receive/have in her fund at the end.

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Financial menu and select TVM or Compound Interest. Enter N=384, I=5, PV=0, FV=754638, P/Y=12, C/Y=1. Solve for PMT.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS], select 'Finance', then 'TVM Solver'. Enter the values and highlight PMT, then press [ALPHA] [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a built-in TVM solver; use the GDC for this calculation.

calculator.description: Calculate the monthly payment (PMT) using the TVM Solver.

highlights.text: earn 5% interest on her funds, compounded annually

steps.title: Solve and round
steps.content:
Using the solver, we find the monthly payment:

$$PMT \approx -817.34...$$

The calculator gives a negative value because this is an 'outflow' (money Yejin pays out of her salary each month). 

The question asks for the answer to the **nearest dollar**.

highlights.text: Give your answer to the nearest dollar

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to the nearest dollar, the amount Yejin needs to save each month is:

$$\$817$$

steps.title: Identify the target future value
steps.content:
From the first part of the problem, we know that to receive a monthly allowance of \$4000 for $30$ years at $5\%$ interest, Yejin needs a lump sum of \$754,638 at the moment she retires (age 60).

This amount is our target **Future Value ($FV$)** for her savings phase.

$$FV = 754,638$$

steps.title: Determine the saving period
steps.content:
Yejin is currently $28$ and wants to retire at $60$. We need to calculate the number of months she will be saving ($n$):

$$\text{Years} = 60 - 28 = 32 \text{ years}$$

Since she saves at the end of each month:

$$n = 32 \times 12 = 384 \text{ months}$$

steps.title: Calculate the monthly interest rate
steps.content:
The interest rate is $5\%$ per annum, but it is **compounded annually**. Because she is making monthly payments, we need to find the equivalent monthly interest rate ($r_m$). 

If the annual multiplier is $1.05$, the monthly multiplier $R$ satisfies:

$$R^{12} = 1.05$$
$$R = (1.05)^{\frac{1}{12}}$$

The effective monthly interest rate is $r_m = R - 1$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1.05^(1/12).

ti84.instructions: Type 1.05^(1/12) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the monthly interest multiplier R.

steps.title: Model savings as geometric series
steps.content:
Let $P$ be the monthly saving amount. Since payments are made at the end of each month, the total sum after $n$ months is a geometric series:

$$FV = P + P(R) + P(R)^2 + \dots + P(R)^{n-1}$$

This is a geometric series where the first term $u_1 = P$ and the common ratio is $R = 1.05^{\frac{1}{12}}$. Using the **sum of a geometric series** formula from the data booklet:

$$S_n = \frac{u_1(r^n - 1)}{r - 1}$$

Substituting our values:

$$754,638 = \frac{P((1.05^{\frac{1}{12}})^{384} - 1)}{1.05^{\frac{1}{12}} - 1}$$

steps.title: Solve for monthly payment
steps.content:
First, simplify the exponent in the numerator:
$$(1.05^{\frac{1}{12}})^{384} = 1.05^{32}$$

Now, rearrange the formula to solve for $P$:

$$P = \frac{754,638 \times (1.05^{\frac{1}{12}} - 1)}{1.05^{32} - 1}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression for P.

calculator.description: Calculate the monthly payment P.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculation:

$$P \approx 816.59...$$

The question asks for the answer to the nearest dollar.

$$P = 817$$

highlights.text: Give your answer to the nearest dollar.

parts.content: Calculate the value of the car after 5 years.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into formula: $V(5) = 20000 \times (1 - 0.15)^5$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify expression: $V(5) = 20000 \times 0.85^5$

- Calculate final answer: $V(5) = 20000 \times 0.4437 = 8874$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify variables and formula
steps.content:
To calculate the value of the car as it depreciates, we use the exponential decay model. This is essentially the compound interest formula found in the **Finance** section of your data booklet, but with a negative interest rate because the value is decreasing.

From the question, we have:
*   Initial Value ($PV$ or $u_1$) = $20,000$
*   Rate of depreciation ($r$) = $15\%$ or $0.15$
*   Time in years ($n$) = $5$

This fits the model:
$$V = PV(1 - r)^n$$

highlights.text: initial value of the car is $20,000
depreciates at a rate of 15% per year

steps.title: Substitute values into formula
steps.content:
We substitute our known values into the depreciation formula. This step is crucial as it earns you the first method mark (**M1**).

$$V = 20,000 \times (1 - 0.15)^5$$

Simplifying the bracket gives us the annual multiplier:
$$V = 20,000 \times (0.85)^5$$

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Now, we perform the calculation. 

$$\begin{aligned} V &= 20,000 \times 0.443705... \\ V &= 8,874.106... \end{aligned}$$

Following the markscheme, we provide the final answer as $8,874$. Note that in most IB exams, unless specified, you should round your final answer to 3 significant figures, which would be $8,870$. However, always check the specific rounding requirements of your markscheme.

steps.title: Understand depreciation as negative interest
steps.content: Hi there! To solve this using the GDC Financial Solver (often called the TVM Solver), we treat the annual depreciation as a negative interest rate. Since the car loses value, the "growth" rate is effectively $-15\%$. This aligns with **Topic 1: Number and Algebra**, specifically the section on financial applications.

highlights.text: depreciates at a rate of 15% per year

steps.title: Identify the solver variables
steps.content:
We need to input the following values into the Financial Solver:

*   $N = 5$ (the number of years)
*   $I\% = -15$ (the annual depreciation rate)
*   $PV = 20,000$ (the initial value of the car)
*   $PMT = 0$ (no additional periodic payments are made)
*   $P/Y = 1$ and $C/Y = 1$ (the depreciation is calculated annually)

We are solving for $FV$ (Future Value), which represents the value of the car after 5 years.

highlights.text: initial value of the car is $20,000

steps.title: Use the Financial Solver
steps.content:
Now, enter these values into your calculator and solve for $FV$. The calculator uses the fundamental logic of the compound interest formula found in your data booklet:

$$FV=PV\times(1+\frac{r}{100k})^{kn}$$

In this case, because it is depreciation, the formula effectively becomes $20000 \times (1 - 0.15)^5$.

steps.title: Define the annual multiplier
steps.content:
To calculate the value iteratively, we first need to determine the value remaining each year. Since the car depreciates by 15% each year, it retains $100\% - 15\% = 85\%$ of its value from the previous year. 

In decimal form, our annual multiplier is:
$$1 - 0.15 = 0.85$$

steps.title: Calculate Years 1 and 2

Starting with the initial value, we multiply by $0.85$ for each year.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Year 1: } & 20,000 \times 0.85 = 17,000 \\ \text{Year 2: } & 17,000 \times 0.85 = 14,450 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Determine the time it takes for the car's value to halve.
parts.markscheme:
- Set up equation: $10000 = 20000 \times 0.85^t$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify equation: $0.5 = 0.85^t$

- Solve for $t$: $t = \frac{\log(0.5)}{\log(0.85)}$

- Calculate final answer: $t \approx 4.27$ years <marks>A1</marks>

<examTip>Remember to include units (years) in your final answer</examTip>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the target value
steps.content: The question asks for the time it takes for the car's value to **halve**. Since the initial value is $20,000$, we are looking for the time $t$ when the value reaches $10,000$.

highlights.text: initial value of the car is $20,000

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
The car depreciates at a rate of $15\%$ per year, meaning it retains $85\%$ ($1 - 0.15 = 0.85$) of its value each year. We can set up our exponential decay equation as follows:

$$10000 = 20000 \times 0.85^t$$

highlights.text: depreciates at a rate of 15% per year

steps.title: Simplify the equation
steps.content:
To isolate the exponential term $0.85^t$, divide both sides by $20,000$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{10000}{20000} &= 0.85^t \\ 0.5 &= 0.85^t \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply logarithms
steps.content:
To solve for $t$ when it is in the exponent, we apply logarithms to both sides. According to the power rule of logarithms, $\log(a^b) = b \log(a)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \log(0.5) &= \log(0.85^t) \\ \log(0.5) &= t \times \log(0.85) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for t
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for $t$ by dividing both sides by $\log(0.85)$:

$$t = \frac{\log(0.5)}{\log(0.85)}$$

steps.title: Calculate and round
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we find the numerical value for $t$:

$$t \approx 4.27 \text{ years}$$

Remember to round to 3 significant figures as is standard for IB Mathematics unless stated otherwise.

gdc.instructions: Enter log(0.5) divided by log(0.85) in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Press [LOG] 0.5 [)] [/] [LOG] 0.85 [)] [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of t using logarithms.

steps.title: Define the target value
steps.content:
To find when the car's value halves, we first need to determine what half of the initial value is. Since the initial value is $20,000$, our target value is:

$$\text{Target Value} = \frac{20000}{2} = 10000$$

We are looking for the time $t$ where the value equals $10,000$.

highlights.text: initial value of the car is $20,000

steps.title: Formulate the depreciation function
steps.content:
The car depreciates at a rate of $15\%$ per year. Using the **Finance** formula from the data booklet for compound interest/depreciation, we find the common ratio (multiplier) is $1 - 0.15 = 0.85$.

$$FV = PV \times \left(1 + \frac{r}{100k}\right)^{kn}$$

In our case, the value function $y$ based on time $x$ (in years) is:

$$y = 20000(0.85)^x$$

highlights.text: depreciates at a rate of 15% per year

steps.title: Set up the graph
steps.content:
Using the **Graphical Method**, we will plot two equations on our calculator and find where they intersect:
1. The depreciation curve: $y_1 = 20000(0.85)^x$
2. The target horizontal line: $y_2 = 10000$

To see the intersection clearly, you'll need to adjust your window settings. A good choice would be $x \in [0, 10]$ and $y \in [0, 25000]$.

The calculator shows the intersection occurs at an $x$-coordinate of approximately $4.27$. This represents the number of years required for the value to reach $10,000$.

Rounding to $3$ significant figures:
$$t \approx 4.27 \text{ years}$$

steps.title: Identify the target value
steps.content:
To find how long it takes for the car's value to halve, we first need to determine the target future value ($FV$). 

Since the initial value is $20,000$, half of that is:

$$\text{Target Value} = \frac{20,000}{2} = 10,000$$

This falls under **Topic 1: Number and Algebra**, specifically **SL 1.4—Financial apps**, where we use technology to solve depreciation problems.

highlights.text: The initial value of the car is $20,000

steps.title: Set up Finance Solver
steps.content:
We will use the **TVM (Time Value of Money) Solver** on the GDC. When using this tool for depreciation, we enter the annual rate as a negative interest percentage. 

Note: In financial solvers, the Present Value ($PV$) and Future Value ($FV$) must have opposite signs to represent the "flow" of value. 

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= 20,000 \\ FV &= -10,000 \\ I\% &= -15 \\ PMT &= 0 \\ P/Y &= 1 \\ C/Y &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

We leave $N$ (the number of years) as the variable we want to solve for.

highlights.text: depreciates at a rate of 15% per year

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now, we use the GDC to solve for $N$. By navigating to the $N$ field and pressing the solve button, the calculator computes the time required for the depreciation to reach the target value.

$$N \approx 4.2650...$$

According to the IB markscheme, we should round this to a sensible level of accuracy (3 significant figures).

2. Select 'Compound Interest' or 'TVM Solver'.
3. Enter: n = (solve), i% = -15, pv = 20000, pmt = 0, fv = -10000, p/y = 1, c/y = 1.
4. Press the function key (e.g., F1) corresponding to 'n' to solve.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS], select 'Finance', then 'TVM Solver'.
2. Set I% = -15

questions.specification:
Bryan decides to purchase a new car with a price of €14 000, but cannot afford the full amount. The car dealership offers two options to finance a loan.

**Finance option A:**

A 6 year loan at a nominal annual interest rate of 14% **compounded quarterly**. No deposit required and repayments are made each quarter.

**Finance option B:**

A 6 year loan at a nominal annual interest rate of $r$% **compounded monthly**. Terms of the loan require a 10% deposit and monthly repayments of €250.

**In this question, give all answers to two decimal places.**

steps.title: Identify loan parameters
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the number of time periods and the interest rate per period for Finance Option A. 

Since the loan is for 6 years with quarterly repayments, the total number of repayments ($n$) is:
$$n = 6 \times 4 = 24$$

The nominal annual interest rate is 14%, compounded quarterly. The interest rate per quarter ($i$) is:
$$i = \frac{14\%}{4} = 3.5\% = 0.035$$

steps.title: Express sum of present values
steps.content:
In the geometric series approach, the original loan amount (€14,000) must equal the sum of the present values of all future repayments ($R$). 

Each repayment is 'discounted' back to the present time. The first repayment is discounted by 1 period, the second by 2, and so on:

$$14000 = \frac{R}{(1.035)^1} + \frac{R}{(1.035)^2} + \dots + \frac{R}{(1.035)^{24}}$$

steps.title: Identify geometric series components
steps.content:
The right side of our equation is a geometric series. To find the sum, we identify the first term ($u_1$), the common ratio ($r$), and the number of terms ($n$):

- First term: $$u_1 = \frac{R}{1.035}$$
- Common ratio: $$r = \frac{1}{1.035}$$
- Number of terms: $$n = 24$$

steps.title: Apply geometric sum formula
steps.content:
Using the geometric sum formula from the data booklet:

$$S_n = \frac{u_1(1 - r^n)}{1 - r}$$

Substituting our values into the equation:

$$14000 = \frac{\frac{R}{1.035}(1 - (\frac{1}{1.035})^{24})}{1 - \frac{1}{1.035}}$$

This simplifies to the standard annuity formula:
$$14000 = R \times \left[ \frac{1 - (1.035)^{-24}}{0.035} \right]$$

steps.title: Calculate the repayment amount
steps.content:
Now we rearrange to solve for $R$:

$$R = \frac{14000 \times 0.035}{1 - (1.035)^{-24}}$$

Using a calculator, we find $R \approx 871.82$.

steps.title: Identify loan variables

To find the total amount paid under **Option A**, we first need to identify the key variables for the loan:

*   **Present Value ($PV$):** The original price of the car is €14,000.

steps.title: Calculate periodic values
steps.content:
We need the interest rate per period ($i$) and the total number of repayment periods ($N$):

*   **Interest rate per quarter ($i$):** 
    $$i = \frac{14\%}{4} = 3.5\% = 0.035$$
*   **Total number of quarters ($N$):** 
    $$N = 6 \times 4 = 24$$

steps.title: Calculate quarterly payment
steps.content:
The **Annuity Formula** relates the Present Value to the periodic payment ($PMT$):

$$PV = PMT \times \frac{1 - (1 + i)^{-N}}{i}$$

Rearranging to solve for $PMT$:

$$PMT = \frac{PV \times i}{1 - (1 + i)^{-N}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$PMT = \frac{14000 \times 0.035}{1 - (1 + 0.035)^{-24}}$$

$$PMT \approx 871.82$$

steps.content:
In Finance Option B, Bryan doesn't borrow the full price of the car. He is required to pay a **10% deposit** upfront. The amount he borrows (the loan balance) is the total price minus this deposit amount. 

We will use the **subtraction method** to find this:

$$\text{Amount borrowed} = 14000 - 1400 = 12600$$

Using the **Complementary Percentage Method**, we calculate the percentage of the car's price that is *not* covered by the deposit. Since the total price represents $100\%$ and the deposit is $10\%$, the portion Bryan needs to borrow is:

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
Depreciation is essentially the opposite of compound interest. We can use the **Compound Interest** formula from the IB Data Booklet.

$$FV = PV \times \left( 1 + \frac{r}{100k} \right)^{kn}$$

Since the depreciation is annual, $k = 1$ (compounded once per year). Because the value is decreasing, we treat the interest rate as negative ($r = -25$).

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values into the formula to find the future value ($FV$):

$$\begin{aligned} FV &= 14000 \times \left(1 - \frac{25}{100}\right)^{6} \\ FV &= 14000 \times (0.75)^{6} \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification:

The firm purchased a new fleet of heavy-duty transport trucks for $240,000$. It is estimated that the value of the fleet depreciates at a constant rate of $12.5\%$ per annum.

Additionally, the firm owns a regional distribution center that was purchased for $650,000$. Exactly $10$ years after the purchase, its appraised value had reduced to $420,000$. Assume the distribution center depreciates at a constant annual rate of $d\%$.

questions.specification:

Let $A_n$ be the amount (in dollars) in Mary's account on the last day of the $n$th month, immediately after the interest had been added.

parts.content: Find an expression for $A_1$ and show that $A_2 = 1.004^2x + 1.004x$.
parts.markscheme:
Find an expression for $A_1$:
$A_1 = 1.004x$ <marks>A1</marks>

Find an expression for $A_2$:
$A_2 = 1.004(1.004x + x)$ <marks>A1</marks>

Simplify to show the result:
$= 1.004^2x + 1.004x$ <marks>AG</marks>

**Note:** Accept an argument in words for example, first deposit has been in for two months and second deposit has been in for one month.

parts.content:
Write down a similar expression for $A_3$ and $A_4$.

Hence show that the amount in Mary's account the day before she turned 10 years old is given by $251(1.004^{120} - 1)x$.
parts.markscheme:
(i)
Find expression for $A_3$:
$A_3 = 1.004(1.004^2x + 1.004x + x) = 1.004^3x + 1.004^2x + 1.004x$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

Find expression for $A_4$:
$A_4 = 1.004^4x + 1.004^3x + 1.004^2x + 1.004x$ <marks>A1</marks>

(ii)
Identify the series for $A_{120}$:
$A_{120} = (1.004^{120} + 1.004^{119} + \ldots + 1.004)x$ <marks>A1</marks>

Use the geometric series formula:
$= \frac{1.004(1.004^{120} - 1)}{1.004 - 1}x$ <marks>M1</marks>

Simplify to show the result:
$= \frac{1.004}{0.004}(1.004^{120} - 1)x$
$= 251(1.004^{120} - 1)x$ <marks>A1 AG</marks>

parts.content: Write down $A_n$ in terms of $x$ for the day before Mary turns 18, stating the value of $n$.
parts.markscheme:
State the expression with the correct value of $n$:
$n = 216$ <marks>A1</marks>
$A_{216} = 251(1.004^{216} - 1)x \quad \left( = x\sum_{t = 1}^{216} 1.004^t \right)$

parts.content: Mary's grandparents wished for the amount in her account to be at least $20\,000$ the day before she was 18. Determine the minimum value of the monthly deposit $x$ required to achieve this. Give your answer correct to the nearest dollar.
parts.markscheme:
Set up the inequality or equation:
$251(1.004^{216} - 1)x = 20\,000$ <marks>A1</marks>

Attempt to solve for $x$:
$x = \frac{20\,000}{251(1.004^{216} - 1)}$ <marks>M1</marks>

State the unrounded value:
$x = 58.22\dots$ <marks>A1</marks>

State the final integer answer:
$x = 59$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Award <marks>A1</marks> for inequality $251(1.004^{216} - 1)x > 20\,000$, <marks>M1</marks> for attempting to solve and <marks>A1</marks> for $x > 58.22\dots$
**Note:** Accept $x = 58$. Accept $x \geqslant 59$.

parts.content: As soon as Mary was 18 she decided to invest $15\,000$ of this money in an account of the same type earning $0.4\%$ interest per month. Interest is added monthly. She withdraws $1000$ once each year on her birthday, immediately after the interest for that month has been added. Determine how many years it will take until there is no money in the account.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the effective annual rate $r$:
$r = 1.004^{12} \quad (= 1.049\dots)$ <marks>M1</marks>

Let $n$ be the number of years after age 18.

Set up the equation for the balance:
$15\,000 r^n - 1000\frac{r^n - 1}{r - 1} = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

Attempt to solve for $n$:
$\Rightarrow n = 27.8\dots$ <marks>M1</marks>

State the value of $n$:
$n = 27.8\dots$ <marks>A1</marks>

State the final integer answer:
$n = 28$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Award <marks>A1</marks> for the equation (with their value of $r$).
**Note:** Accept $n = 27$.

steps.content:
First, let's identify the values given in the problem for Gen's investment:

  • Substitute the correct values: $\frac{3552.59}{(1.015)^{10}}$ OR $2400 \times \left(\frac{1.04}{1.015}\right)^{10}$ A1

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, let's list the information we have for the investment and the economic conditions:

*   Principal ($PV$): $2400$
*   Annual Interest Rate ($r$): $4\%$
*   Compounding periods per year ($k$): $1$ (compounded annually)
*   Time ($n$): $10$ years
*   Inflation Rate: $1.5\%$

This question falls under **Topic 1: Number and Algebra**, specifically the financial applications of compound interest.

steps.title: Calculate the nominal future value
steps.content:
The first step in our approach is to find the **nominal value** (the actual amount in the bank) after 10 years. We use the compound interest formula from the data booklet:

$$FV=PV\times(1+\frac{r}{100k})^{kn}$$

Substituting our values:

$$FV_{nominal} = 2400 \times (1 + \frac{4}{100 \times 1})^{1 \times 10} = 2400 \times (1.04)^{10}$$

$$FV_{nominal} \approx 3552.59$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Run-Matrix menu and enter the expression.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the nominal future value.

steps.title: Calculate the real value
steps.content:
The **real value** represents the purchasing power of that money, adjusted for inflation. To find this, we discount the nominal future value using the inflation rate over the same period:

$$\text{Real Value} = \frac{FV_{nominal}}{(1 + \text{inflation rate})^n}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Real Value} = \frac{3552.59}{(1 + 0.015)^{10}} = \frac{3552.59}{(1.015)^{10}}$$

$$\text{Real Value} \approx 3061.15$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculator.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the division expression.

calculator.description: Divide the nominal value by the inflation factor.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The real value of Gen's savings after 10 years, rounded to two decimal places as requested, is:

$$\text{Real Value} = \$3061.15$$

highlights.text: Give your answers to this question correct to two decimal places.

steps.title: Identify growth factors
steps.content:
To find the real value of an investment, we need to compare how fast the money grows compared to how fast prices rise. We can represent these as growth factors:

- **Interest Growth Factor**: Since the interest rate is $4\%$, the money grows by a factor of $1 + \frac{4}{100} = 1.04$.
- **Inflation Growth Factor**: Since the inflation rate is $1.5\%$, the purchasing power decreases, or prices rise by a factor of $1 + \frac{1.5}{100} = 1.015$.

highlights.text: rate of inflation during this 10 year period is 1.5% per year

steps.title: Apply the Integrated Ratio
steps.content:
Using the **Integrated Ratio Approach**, we calculate the real value in a single step. We multiply the initial investment by the ratio of the interest growth factor to the inflation growth factor, raised to the power of the number of years ($n = 10$).

$$\text{Real Value} = PV \times \left( \frac{1 + \text{interest rate}}{1 + \text{inflation rate}} \right)^n$$

Substituting our values:
$$\text{Real Value} = 2400 \times \left( \frac{1.04}{1.015} \right)^{10}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, we perform the calculation. The ratio $\frac{1.04}{1.015}$ represents the "real" growth factor per year. When we raise this to the power of $10$ and multiply by the principal, we get the real value in today's terms.

$$\text{Real Value} \approx 3061.147...$$

steps.title: Identify the two-step strategy
steps.content:
To find the **real value** of the savings, we need to account for two different rates:
1. The **investment rate** (4%) which increases the amount of money in the account.
2. The **inflation rate** (1.5%) which decreases the purchasing power of that money.

We will first find the nominal amount of money Gen has after 10 years, then we will "discount" that amount using the inflation rate to find its value in today's terms.

steps.title: Discount for inflation
steps.content:
The value we just found ($3552.59$) is the "nominal" amount. To find the **real value**, we use the inflation rate as a discount rate. 

We perform a second TVM calculation. This time, we set the result from the first step as our $FV$ and solve for $PV$ using the inflation rate as $I\%$. This tells us what that future amount is worth in today's dollars.

Set the following parameters:
$$\begin{aligned} N &= 10 \\ I\% &= 1.5 \\ PMT &= 0 \\ FV &= 3552.59 \\ P/Y &= 1 \\ C/Y &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $PV$ gives the real value of the savings.

steps.content:
